content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

January 4, 1977


Mr. John F. Mudge
City Superintendent
Santa Maria School District
Post Office Box 460
Santa Maria, California 93454

Dear Mr. Mudge:

     This is in response to your letter of December 27, 1976,
requesting advice regarding whether teachers in your District
should be included in the Conflict of Interest Code appropriate
for the District.

     In your letter, you set forth certain examples of teachers
in your district who are involved in decision making and also
have commercial interests in such matters. I will not make
specific determinations with respect to the four examples
given, but rather will address generally the considerations
which should be undertaken in designating teachers in a
Conflict of Interest Code.

     It has generally been the view of this division of the
Commission that academics should not be included within Con-
flict of Interest Codes because (1) teachers do not make or
participate in the making of governmental decisions within
the meaning of the Political Reform Act or the Commission's
regulations 1/; and (2) any decisions in which teachers nor-
mally participate would not foreseeably have a material fin-
ancial effect upon any private interests. 2/ (See Government
Code §87302(a).


1/See Commission Regulation defining "Making or
Participating in the Making of a Governmental Decision",
2 Cal. Adm. Code §§18700(b), (c), attached.

2/See Commission Regulation defining "Material
Financial Effect", 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702, attached.

John F. Mudge
January 4, 1977                                                Page Two


     There is, of course, a further reason for not generally
designating teachers for purposes of Conflict of Interest
Codes, and that is to prevent unwarranted intrusion into the
classroom and academic atmosphere.

     Where, however, it is clear that there has been a
significant delegation of authority to a teacher to make
or participate in the making of District decisions which
could have a material financial effect upon private
interests, the District quite clearly may exercise its
authority pursuant to Government Code §87302 to include
such teachers within its Conflict of Interest Code.
Likewise, if a teacher's participation in such decisions
is sporadic or ad hoc in nature, the District might
properly exercise its discretion to leave such teachers
out of its Code.  The issue really focuses on what is
foreseeable at the time the District develops its Code
or is faced with a situation of changing circumstances
with respect to the delegation of authority discussed
above.

     The Commission has issued a statement of policy
regarding the concept "foreseeable", and that statement,
as well as the Thorner opinion mentioned therein, is
attached.

     Regardless of a teacher's inclusion in or exclusion
from a Conflict of Interest Code, the general prohibitions
of the Political Reform Act (Government Code §§87100 and
87103) prevent a public school teacher from making or
participating in the making of a decision which would have
a material financial effect on one of his or her interests.
3/  In this respect, the District may be interested to
know that the remedy for violation of Government Code
§87100 is an injunction to restrain the execution of any
official action in relation to which the violation occurs.
(See Government Code §91003.)


_________________________________________________________________
3/See Government Code §§87100 and 87103.
     See also, attached letter to Mr. Art Campos.
     This letter is not a Commission decision, but
     is informal staff advice.

John F. Mudge
January 4, 1977                                          Page Three


     The foregoing constitute the considerations that the
Board must weigh in developing its Conflict of Interest
Code. At least in the first instance, such decisions are
for the Board to decide. The hearings on the Code before
the Board and its code reviewing body should provide a
record justifying the determination to include or exclude
teachers or.

     If I can be of further assistance to you, please feel
free to call me at 916/322-6444 in Sacramento.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [signature]
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division


DLS:glb
Enclosures

sections.question: Whether teachers in the Santa Maria School District should be included in the Conflict of Interest Code appropriate for the District, with specific examples of teachers involved in decision making who have commercial interests in such matters.
sections.conclusion: It has generally been the view of this division of the Commission that academics should not be included within Conflict of Interest Codes because (1) teachers do not make or participate in the making of governmental decisions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act or the Commission's regulations; and (2) any decisions in which teachers normally participate would not foreseeably have a material financial effect upon any private interests. However, where it is clear that there has been a significant delegation of authority to a teacher to make or participate in the making of District decisions which could have a material financial effect upon private interests, the District may exercise its authority to include such teachers within its Conflict of Interest Code. Regardless of inclusion or exclusion, the general prohibitions of the Political Reform Act prevent a public school teacher from making or participating in the making of a decision which would have a material financial effect on one of his or her interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Should teachers in a school district be included in the district's Conflict of Interest Code when they participate in decision-making and have commercial interests potentially affected by those decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Teachers should generally not be included in Conflict of Interest Codes because they do not typically make governmental decisions with material financial effects. However, if teachers are clearly delegated significant authority over decisions with material financial effects, they may be included. In all cases, the Political Reform Act's general prohibitions apply regardless of code inclusion.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present; question and conclusion extracted from body text. Document references attached enclosures and footnotes. Informal staff advice, not a formal Commission decision. Includes regulatory citations and cross-references.

QUESTION: Whether teachers in the Santa Maria School District should be included in the Conflict of Interest Code appropriate for the District, with specific examples of teachers involved in decision making who have commercial interests in such matters.

CONCLUSION: It has generally been the view of this division of the Commission that academics should not be included within Conflict of Interest Codes because (1) teachers do not make or participate in the making of governmental decisions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act or the Commission's regulations; and (2) any decisions in which teachers normally participate would not foreseeably have a material financial effect upon any private interests. However, where it is clear that there has been a significant delegation of authority to a teacher to make or participate in the making of District decisions which could have a material financial effect upon private interests, the District may exercise its authority to include such teachers within its Conflict of Interest Code. Regardless of inclusion or exclusion, the general prohibitions of the Political Reform Act prevent a public school teacher from making or participating in the making of a decision which would have a material financial effect on one of his or her interests.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the Santa Maria School District on whether to include teachers in its Conflict of Interest Code. The response establishes that teachers should generally be excluded to avoid intrusion into academia, but may be included when they have significant delegated authority over decisions with material financial effects, with the determination left to the Board's discretion.
